      1090
      1091
      1092  # =============================================================================
      1093 +# API ROUTES — BUNDLE EXPORT/IMPORT (.portolan)
      1094 +# =============================================================================
      1095 +
      1096 +@app.get("/api/documents/{doc_id}/export-bundle")
      1097 +async def export_bundle(doc_id: int):
      1098 +    """
      1099 +    Export a portable .portolan bundle for a document.
      1100 +
      1101 +    A .portolan file is a ZIP archive containing the original PDF, all markup
      1102 +    data, layer configuration, and scale settings. Recipients can import the
      1103 +    bundle to restore the full session — PDF + markups + layers — on any
      1104 +    PortolanCAST installation without needing access to the original file.
      1105 +
      1106 +    Bundle contents:
      1107 +        metadata.json  — app version, filename, page count, export timestamp
      1108 +        original.pdf   — byte-exact copy of the source PDF
      1109 +        markups.json   — { pages: { "0": fabricJSON, ... } }
      1110 +        layers.json    — { layers: [...], activeId }
      1111 +        scale.json     — { preset, paper_inches_per_unit, unit_label }
      1112 +
      1113 +    Security:
      1114 +        - Validates document + PDF file exist before building the bundle
      1115 +        - In-memory ZIP — no temp files written to disk
      1116 +        - Markup/layer/scale data is read from DB, never from request body
      1117 +
      1118 +    Args:
      1119 +        doc_id: Document database ID.
      1120 +
      1121 +    Returns:
      1122 +        ZIP file as application/zip with Content-Disposition attachment.
      1123 +    """
      1124 +    doc = db.get_document(doc_id)
      1125 +    if not doc:
      1126 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Document not found")
      1127 +
      1128 +    pdf_path = Path(doc["filepath"])
      1129 +    if not pdf_path.exists():
      1130 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="PDF file missing from disk")
      1131 +
      1132 +    # Load all document state from DB
      1133 +    markups = db.get_markups(doc_id)
      1134 +    pages_str = {str(k): v for k, v in markups.items()}
      1135 +
      1136 +    raw_layers = db.get_document_setting(doc_id, "layers")
      1137 +    if raw_layers:
      1138 +        try:
      1139 +            layers_obj = _json_top.loads(raw_layers)
      1140 +        except Exception:
      1141 +            layers_obj = _default_layers()
      1142 +    else:
      1143 +        layers_obj = _default_layers()
      1144 +
      1145 +    raw_scale = db.get_document_setting(doc_id, "scale")
      1146 +    if raw_scale:
      1147 +        try:
      1148 +            scale_obj = _json_top.loads(raw_scale)
      1149 +        except Exception:
      1150 +            scale_obj = _default_scale()
      1151 +    else:
      1152 +        scale_obj = _default_scale()
      1153 +
      1154 +    metadata = {
      1155 +        "version": "1",
      1156 +        "app": "PortolanCAST",
      1157 +        "filename": doc["filename"],
      1158 +        "page_count": doc["page_count"],
      1159 +        "exported_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
      1160 +    }
      1161 +
      1162 +    # Build ZIP in memory — avoids writing temp files to disk
      1163 +    buf = io.BytesIO()
      1164 +    with zipfile.ZipFile(buf, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:
      1165 +        zf.writestr("metadata.json", _json_top.dumps(metadata, indent=2))
      1166 +        zf.write(str(pdf_path), "original.pdf")
      1167 +        zf.writestr("markups.json", _json_top.dumps({"pages": pages_str}, indent=2))
      1168 +        zf.writestr("layers.json", _json_top.dumps(layers_obj, indent=2))
      1169 +        zf.writestr("scale.json", _json_top.dumps(scale_obj, indent=2))
      1170 +
      1171 +    # Build download filename: strip .pdf extension, add .portolan
      1172 +    base_name = doc["filename"]
      1173 +    if base_name.lower().endswith(".pdf"):
      1174 +        base_name = base_name[:-4]
      1175 +    download_name = base_name + ".portolan"
      1176 +
      1177 +    return Response(
      1178 +        content=buf.getvalue(),
      1179 +        media_type="application/zip",
      1180 +        headers={
      1181 +            "Content-Disposition": f'attachment; filename="{download_name}"',
      1182 +        },
      1183 +    )
      1184 +
      1185 +
      1186 +@app.post("/api/documents/import-bundle")
      1187 +async def import_bundle(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
      1188 +    """
      1189 +    Import a .portolan bundle and restore the document + all markup state.
      1190 +
      1191 +    Reads the ZIP, validates required members, saves the PDF, creates a new
      1192 +    document record, and restores markups/layers/scale from the bundle.
      1193 +    Always creates a NEW document — never overwrites an existing one.
      1194 +
      1195 +    Expected bundle (minimum required):
      1196 +        metadata.json  — must be present (version check future-proofing)
      1197 +        original.pdf   — PDF bytes; must start with %PDF- magic bytes
      1198 +
      1199 +    Optional (imported if present, defaults used if absent):
      1200 +        markups.json, layers.json, scale.json
      1201 +
      1202 +    Security:
      1203 +        - Extension must be .portolan
      1204 +        - Size limited by MAX_UPLOAD_SIZE constant
      1205 +        - zipfile.BadZipFile caught to reject corrupt or spoofed ZIPs
      1206 +        - PDF magic bytes validated before saving
      1207 +        - Optional members are schema-validated before DB write
      1208 +
      1209 +    Args:
      1210 +        file: The uploaded .portolan bundle (multipart form).
      1211 +
      1212 +    Returns:
      1213 +        { id, filename, page_count, redirect }
      1214 +    """
      1215 +    # SECURITY: extension check — only .portolan bundles accepted here
      1216 +    if not file.filename or not file.filename.lower().endswith(".portolan"):
      1217 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="File must be a .portolan bundle")
      1218 +
      1219 +    raw = await file.read()
      1220 +
      1221 +    # SECURITY: size limit
      1222 +    if len(raw) > MAX_UPLOAD_SIZE:
      1223 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=413, detail="Bundle exceeds maximum upload size")
      1224 +
      1225 +    # SECURITY: reject corrupt or non-ZIP files
      1226 +    try:
      1227 +        with zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(raw)) as zf:
      1228 +            namelist = zf.namelist()
      1229 +
      1230 +            # Required members
      1231 +            if "metadata.json" not in namelist:
      1232 +                raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Bundle missing metadata.json")
      1233 +            if "original.pdf" not in namelist:
      1234 +                raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Bundle missing original.pdf")
      1235 +
      1236 +            pdf_bytes = zf.read("original.pdf")
      1237 +
      1238 +            # SECURITY: validate PDF magic bytes
      1239 +            if not pdf_bytes.startswith(b"%PDF-"):
      1240 +                raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Bundle contains invalid PDF")
      1241 +
      1242 +            # Read metadata to get original filename
      1243 +            try:
      1244 +                meta = _json_top.loads(zf.read("metadata.json"))
      1245 +            except Exception:
      1246 +                meta = {}
      1247 +            original_filename = str(meta.get("filename", file.filename))
      1248 +
      1249 +            # Read optional members — use defaults if absent
      1250 +            markups_pages = {}
      1251 +            if "markups.json" in namelist:
      1252 +                try:
      1253 +                    markups_data = _json_top.loads(zf.read("markups.json"))
      1254 +                    markups_pages = markups_data.get("pages", {})
      1255 +                    if not isinstance(markups_pages, dict):
      1256 +                        markups_pages = {}
      1257 +                except Exception:
      1258 +                    markups_pages = {}
      1259 +
      1260 +            layers_raw = None
      1261 +            if "layers.json" in namelist:
      1262 +                try:
      1263 +                    layers_obj = _json_top.loads(zf.read("layers.json"))
      1264 +                    if isinstance(layers_obj, dict) and "layers" in layers_obj:
      1265 +                        layers_raw = _json_top.dumps(layers_obj)
      1266 +                except Exception:
      1267 +                    pass
      1268 +
      1269 +            scale_raw = None
      1270 +            if "scale.json" in namelist:
      1271 +                try:
      1272 +                    scale_obj = _json_top.loads(zf.read("scale.json"))
      1273 +                    if isinstance(scale_obj, dict) and "preset" in scale_obj:
      1274 +                        scale_raw = _json_top.dumps(scale_obj)
      1275 +                except Exception:
      1276 +                    pass
      1277 +
      1278 +    except HTTPException:
      1279 +        raise  # re-raise our own validation errors
      1280 +    except zipfile.BadZipFile:
      1281 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="File is not a valid ZIP/portolan bundle")
      1282 +    except Exception as e:
      1283 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=f"Bundle read error: {e}")
      1284 +
      1285 +    # Save PDF to projects directory with a unique filename
      1286 +    pdf_filename = uuid.uuid4().hex + ".pdf"
      1287 +    pdf_dest = PROJECTS_DIR / pdf_filename
      1288 +    PROJECTS_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
      1289 +    pdf_dest.write_bytes(pdf_bytes)
      1290 +
      1291 +    # Validate + measure the PDF
      1292 +    try:
      1293 +        pdf_info = pdf_engine.get_pdf_info(str(pdf_dest))
      1294 +    except Exception as e:
      1295 +        pdf_dest.unlink(missing_ok=True)
      1296 +        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail=f"PDF validation failed: {e}")
      1297 +
      1298 +    file_size = len(pdf_bytes)
      1299 +    page_count = pdf_info.get("page_count", 1)
      1300 +
      1301 +    # Create document record in DB
      1302 +    new_doc_id = db.add_document(original_filename, str(pdf_dest), page_count, file_size)
      1303 +
      1304 +    # Restore optional state
      1305 +    if markups_pages:
      1306 +        db.save_markups(new_doc_id, markups_pages)
      1307 +    if layers_raw:
      1308 +        db.set_document_setting(new_doc_id, "layers", layers_raw)
      1309 +    if scale_raw:
      1310 +        db.set_document_setting(new_doc_id, "scale", scale_raw)
      1311 +
      1312 +    return JSONResponse({
      1313 +        "id": new_doc_id,
      1314 +        "filename": original_filename,
      1315 +        "page_count": page_count,
      1316 +        "redirect": f"/edit/{new_doc_id}",
      1317 +    })
      1318 +
      1319 +
      1320 +# =============================================================================
      1321  # ENTRY POINT
      1322  # =============================================================================
      1323

      1504 +            alert('Please select a PDF or .portolan bundle file.');

● Update(/mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/OpenRevu/static/js/toolbar.js)
  ⎿  Added 113 lines
      1536              console.error('Upload failed:', err);
      1537          }
      1538      }
      1539 +
      1540 +    // =========================================================================
      1541 +    // BUNDLE SAVE/IMPORT (.portolan)
      1542 +    // =========================================================================
      1543 +
      1544 +    /**
      1545 +     * Save a .portolan bundle for the current document.
      1546 +     *
      1547 +     * Saves current markups first (same pattern as _handleExport), then calls
      1548 +     * GET /api/documents/{id}/export-bundle and triggers a browser download.
      1549 +     * The bundle preserves the original PDF + all markups, layers, and scale
      1550 +     * settings so the document can be fully re-edited on any PortolanCAST
      1551 +     * installation.
      1552 +     */
      1553 +    async _handleBundleSave() {
      1554 +        if (!this.viewer || !this.viewer.docId) {
      1555 +            alert('No document open to save as bundle.');
      1556 +            return;
      1557 +        }
      1558 +
      1559 +        const statusMsg = document.getElementById('status-message');
      1560 +        statusMsg.textContent = 'Saving bundle...';
      1561 +
      1562 +        try {
      1563 +            // Save current page state to in-memory map before bundling
      1564 +            if (this.canvas && this.canvas.onContentChange) {
      1565 +                this.canvas.onPageChanging(this.viewer.currentPage);
      1566 +            }
      1567 +
      1568 +            // Flush all markups to the server so the bundle has current data
      1569 +            const pages = this.canvas ? this.canvas.getAllPageMarkups() : {};
      1570 +            const saveResp = await fetch(
      1571 +                `/api/documents/${this.viewer.docId}/markups`,
      1572 +                {
      1573 +                    method: 'PUT',
      1574 +                    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      1575 +                    body: JSON.stringify({ pages }),
      1576 +                }
      1577 +            );
      1578 +            if (!saveResp.ok) {
      1579 +                throw new Error('Failed to save markups before bundling');
      1580 +            }
      1581 +
      1582 +            // Fetch the bundle as a binary blob
      1583 +            const bundleResp = await fetch(
      1584 +                `/api/documents/${this.viewer.docId}/export-bundle`
      1585 +            );
      1586 +            if (!bundleResp.ok) {
      1587 +                const err = await bundleResp.json();
      1588 +                throw new Error(err.detail || 'Bundle export failed');
      1589 +            }
      1590 +
      1591 +            const blob = await bundleResp.blob();
      1592 +
      1593 +            // Trigger browser download using the server-provided filename
      1594 +            const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
      1595 +            const a = document.createElement('a');
      1596 +            a.href = url;
      1597 +            const cd = bundleResp.headers.get('Content-Disposition');
      1598 +            const match = cd && cd.match(/filename="?([^"]+)"?/);
      1599 +            a.download = match ? match[1] : 'document.portolan';
      1600 +            document.body.appendChild(a);
      1601 +            a.click();
      1602 +            document.body.removeChild(a);
      1603 +            URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
      1604 +
      1605 +            statusMsg.textContent = 'Bundle saved';
      1606 +        } catch (err) {
      1607 +            statusMsg.textContent = `Bundle save error: ${err.message}`;
      1608 +            console.error('[Toolbar] Bundle save failed:', err);
      1609 +        }
      1610 +    }
      1611 +
      1612 +    /**
      1613 +     * Import a .portolan bundle and open the restored document.
      1614 +     *
      1615 +     * POSTs the bundle file to /api/documents/import-bundle, which creates
      1616 +     * a new document record and restores all markup/layer/scale state.
      1617 +     * On success, navigates to the new document's editor page.
      1618 +     *
      1619 +     * Args:
      1620 +     *   file: File object from the file input (must end with .portolan).
      1621 +     */
      1622 +    async _handleBundleImport(file) {
      1623 +        const statusMsg = document.getElementById('status-message');
      1624 +        statusMsg.textContent = `Importing ${file.name}...`;
      1625 +
      1626 +        const formData = new FormData();
      1627 +        formData.append('file', file);
      1628 +
      1629 +        try {
      1630 +            const resp = await fetch('/api/documents/import-bundle', {
      1631 +                method: 'POST',
      1632 +                body: formData,
      1633 +            });
      1634 +
      1635 +            if (!resp.ok) {
      1636 +                const err = await resp.json();
      1637 +                throw new Error(err.detail || 'Import failed');
      1638 +            }
      1639 +
      1640 +            const result = await resp.json();
      1641 +            statusMsg.textContent = `Imported: ${result.filename}`;
      1642 +
      1643 +            // Navigate to the newly created document
      1644 +            window.history.pushState({}, '', `/edit/${result.id}`);
      1645 +            await this.viewer.loadDocument(result.id);
      1646 +
      1647 +        } catch (err) {
      1648 +            statusMsg.textContent = `Import error: ${err.message}`;
      1649 +            console.error('[Toolbar] Bundle import failed:', err);
      1650 +        }
      1651 +    }
      1652  }

● Both endpoints work. Now write the test file:

  ★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────
  The test structure mirrors the plan exactly: UI Shell tests verify static HTML presence (fast, no JS eval needed),
  then endpoint tests hit the API directly from Node.js fetch, and the round-trip test verifies semantic equivalence —
  the imported page_count must match the exported page_count. This pyramid (HTML → API → round-trip) catches failures at
   progressively higher integration levels.
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

● Write(/mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/OpenRevu/test_bundle.mjs)
  ⎿  Wrote 197 lines to /mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/OpenRevu/test_bundle.mjs
        1 /**
        2  * PortolanCAST — Bundle Export/Import Browser Tests
        3  *
        4  * Tests the .portolan ZIP bundle format:
        5  *   - UI presence of Save Bundle button and updated file-input accept
        6  *   - GET /api/documents/{id}/export-bundle endpoint (HTTP, content-type, filename, contents)
        7  *   - POST /api/documents/import-bundle round-trip (import → doc accessible)
        8  *
        9  * Run via Windows Chrome from WSL:
       10  *   cmd.exe /c "cd C:\Users\User1\ClaudeProjects\OpenRevu && node test_bundle.mjs"
       11  *
       12  * Author: PortolanCAST
       13  * Version: 1.0.0
       14  * Date: 2026-02-24
       15  */
       16
       17 import { chromium } from 'playwright';
       18 import { createWriteStream } from 'fs';
       19 import { unlink, readFile, writeFile } from 'fs/promises';
       20 import path from 'path';
       21 import os from 'os';
       22
       23 const BASE_URL = 'http://127.0.0.1:8000';
       24 const DOC_ID = 1;
       25 let passed = 0;
       26 let failed = 0;
       27
       28 function assert(condition, msg) {
       29     if (condition) {
       30         passed++;
       31         console.log(`  PASS: ${msg}`);
       32     } else {
       33         failed++;
       34         console.log(`  FAIL: ${msg}`);
       35     }
       36 }
       37
       38 async function run() {
       39     const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: true });
       40     const page = await browser.newPage();
       41
       42     // Temp file paths used by export+import round-trip
       43     const bundlePath = path.join(os.tmpdir(), `test_portolan_${Date.now()}.portolan`);
       44     let importedDocId = null;
       45
       46     try {
       47         // =====================================================================
       48         // Group 1: UI Shell (2 tests)
       49         // =====================================================================
       50         console.log('\n  -- Group 1: UI Shell --');
       51
       52         await page.goto(`${BASE_URL}/edit/${DOC_ID}`, { waitUntil: 'networkidle' });
       53         await page.waitForTimeout(1200);
       54
       55         // Test 1: #btn-save-bundle exists inside .toolbar-export
       56         const bundleBtnInExport = await page.evaluate(() => {
       57             const btn = document.getElementById('btn-save-bundle');
       58             if (!btn) return false;
       59             const parent = btn.closest('.toolbar-export');
       60             return parent !== null;
       61         });
       62         assert(bundleBtnInExport, '#btn-save-bundle exists inside .toolbar-export');
       63
       64         // Test 2: #file-input accept attribute includes .portolan
       65         const fileInputAccept = await page.evaluate(() => {
       66             const input = document.getElementById('file-input');
       67             return input ? input.getAttribute('accept') : '';
       68         });
       69         assert(
       70             fileInputAccept && fileInputAccept.includes('.portolan'),
       71             `#file-input accept contains .portolan (got "${fileInputAccept}")`
       72         );
       73
       74         // =====================================================================
       75         // Group 2: Export Bundle Endpoint (5 tests)
       76         // =====================================================================
       77         console.log('\n  -- Group 2: Export Bundle Endpoint --');
       78
       79         // Test 3: GET /api/documents/1/export-bundle → 200
       80         const exportResp = await fetch(`${BASE_URL}/api/documents/${DOC_ID}/export-bundle`);
       81         assert(exportResp.status === 200, `GET /api/documents/${DOC_ID}/export-bundle → 200`);
       82
       83         // Test 4: Content-Type is application/zip
       84         const contentType = exportResp.headers.get('content-type') || '';
       85         assert(
       86             contentType.includes('application/zip'),
       87             `Content-Type is application/zip (got "${contentType}")`
       88         );
       89
       90         // Test 5: Content-Disposition filename contains .portolan
       91         const cd = exportResp.headers.get('content-disposition') || '';
       92         assert(
       93             cd.includes('.portolan'),
       94             `Content-Disposition contains .portolan (got "${cd}")`
       95         );
       96
       97         // Download the bundle to disk for inspection and re-import
       98         const bundleBytes = await exportResp.arrayBuffer();
       99         await writeFile(bundlePath, Buffer.from(bundleBytes));
      100
      101         // Test 6: ZIP namelist contains all 5 required files
      102         // Use Python to inspect the ZIP (Node stdlib has no built-in ZIP reader)
      103         const { execSync } = await import('child_process');
      104         const zipContents = execSync(
      105             `python3 -c "import zipfile,json; zf=zipfile.ZipFile('${bundlePath}'); print(json.dumps(sor
          ted(zf.namelist())))"`
      106         ).toString().trim();
      107         const zipFiles = JSON.parse(zipContents);
      108         const requiredFiles = ['metadata.json', 'original.pdf', 'markups.json', 'layers.json', 'scale.j
          son'];
      109         const allPresent = requiredFiles.every(f => zipFiles.includes(f));
      110         assert(allPresent, `ZIP contains all 5 required files (got ${zipFiles.join(', ')})`);
      111
      112         // Test 7: metadata.json has version, filename, page_count fields
      113         const metaJson = execSync(
      114             `python3 -c "import zipfile,json; zf=zipfile.ZipFile('${bundlePath}'); print(zf.read('metad
          ata.json').decode())"`
      115         ).toString().trim();
      116         const meta = JSON.parse(metaJson);
      117         assert(
      118             meta.version && meta.filename && meta.page_count,
      119             `metadata.json has version="${meta.version}", filename="${meta.filename}", page_count=${met
          a.page_count}`
      120         );
      121
      122         // =====================================================================
      123         // Group 3: Import Round-trip (3 tests)
      124         // =====================================================================
      125         console.log('\n  -- Group 3: Import Round-trip --');
      126
      127         // Test 8: POST exported bundle to /api/documents/import-bundle → 200 with id
      128         // Use FormData + fetch from Node (Node 18+ supports FormData natively)
      129         const { readFile: readFileCb } = await import('fs/promises');
      130         const bundleBuffer = await readFileCb(bundlePath);
      131         const formData = new FormData();
      132         const blob = new Blob([bundleBuffer], { type: 'application/zip' });
      133         formData.append('file', blob, 'test.portolan');
      134
      135         const importResp = await fetch(`${BASE_URL}/api/documents/import-bundle`, {
      136             method: 'POST',
      137             body: formData,
      138         });
      139         assert(importResp.status === 200, `POST /api/documents/import-bundle → 200`);
      140
      141         let importResult = null;
      142         if (importResp.ok) {
      143             importResult = await importResp.json();
      144             importedDocId = importResult.id;
      145         }
      146         assert(
      147             importResult && typeof importResult.id === 'number',
      148             `Import returns { id: ${importResult?.id} }`
      149         );
      150
      151         // Test 9: GET /edit/{id} → 200 (imported doc accessible)
      152         if (importedDocId) {
      153             const editResp = await fetch(`${BASE_URL}/edit/${importedDocId}`);
      154             assert(editResp.status === 200, `GET /edit/${importedDocId} → 200 (imported doc accessible)
          `);
      155         } else {
      156             failed++;
      157             console.log('  FAIL: /edit/{id} check skipped — import did not return id');
      158         }
      159
      160         // Test 10: Imported doc page_count matches original
      161         if (importedDocId) {
      162             const infoResp = await fetch(`${BASE_URL}/api/documents/${importedDocId}/info`);
      163             const info = await infoResp.json();
      164             assert(
      165                 info.page_count === meta.page_count,
      166                 `Imported page_count (${info.page_count}) matches original (${meta.page_count})`
      167             );
      168         } else {
      169             failed++;
      170             console.log('  FAIL: page_count check skipped — import did not return id');
      171         }
      172
      173     } finally {
      174         await browser.close();
      175
      176         // Cleanup temp bundle file
      177         try { await unlink(bundlePath); } catch (_) {}
      178
      179         // Delete the imported test document to keep DB clean
      180         if (importedDocId) {
      181             try {
      182                 await fetch(`${BASE_URL}/api/documents/${importedDocId}`, { method: 'DELETE' });
      183             } catch (_) {}
      184         }
      185
      186         console.log('\n══════════════════════════════════════════════════');
      187         console.log(`  Results: ${passed} passed, ${failed} failed, ${passed + failed} total`);
      188         console.log('══════════════════════════════════════════════════');
      189
      190         if (failed > 0) process.exit(1);
      191     }
      192 }
      193
      194 run().catch(err => {
      195     console.error('Fatal test error:', err);
      196     process.exit(1);
      197 });

  No ZIP library in node_modules. I need to implement ZIP inspection in pure Node.js. ZIP files have a specific format I
   can parse manually, or I can take a different approach for tests 6 and 7.

  The approach: Instead of using Python or a ZIP library, I can validate the ZIP structure by:
  1. Verifying the file starts with the ZIP magic bytes PK (0x50 0x4B)
  2. Posting the bundle to the import endpoint (which validates structure server-side) and checking the response - this
  implicitly validates the ZIP contains metadata.json and original.pdf
  3. Then fetching the original doc's info and the imported doc's info to validate page_count matches

  4. For test 6, I'll just check the magic bytes and confirm the import succeeds, which proves the ZIP structure is
  valid since the server validates it.
  5. For test 7, I'll verify the metadata fields directly from the import response.

  Actually, I could implement a minimal ZIP central directory reader in JavaScript to parse the structure myself, but
  that's getting complex. Let me reconsider using WSL Python instead—I can call wsl python3 directly from Node's
  execSync on Windows, which might be simpler than building a ZIP parser. Or even better, Node has zlib built-in, so I
  could decompress the ZIP without external dependencies.

  Actually, the cleanest approach is to validate the bundle through the server's import endpoint first, then cross-check
   the response fields against the metadata. For the ZIP contents check, I'll try calling WSL Python from Node since
  it's available on the system.

● No zip library available. I'll rewrite the ZIP inspection tests using pure Node.js binary parsing (ZIP Local File
  Headers are parseable) to avoid the Windows python3 dependency:

● Write(/mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/OpenRevu/test_bundle.mjs)

      10 + * the ZIP Local File Header structures directly from the binary buffer.
      11 + *
      12   * Run via Windows Chrome from WSL:
      13   *   cmd.exe /c "cd C:\Users\User1\ClaudeProjects\OpenRevu && node test_bundle.mjs"
      14   *
     ...
      18   */

     ...
      37      }
      38  }
      39
      40 +/**
      41 + * Parse filenames from a ZIP buffer using Local File Header signatures.
      42 + *
      43 + * ZIP Local File Header format (per PKZIP spec):
      44 + *   Offset  Length  Field
      45 + *   0       4       Signature: 0x04034b50 (little-endian PK\x03\x04)
      46 + *   ...
      47 + *   26      2       Filename length (n)
      48 + *   28      2       Extra field length (m)
      49 + *   30      n       Filename
      50 + *   30+n    m       Extra field
      51 + *   30+n+m  ...     File data
      52 + *
      53 + * We walk the buffer looking for each PK\x03\x04 signature to collect names.
      54 + *
      55 + * Args:
      56 + *   buf: Node.js Buffer of the full ZIP file.
      57 + *
      58 + * Returns:
      59 + *   Array of filename strings found in the local file headers.
      60 + */
      61 +function zipFilenames(buf) {
      62 +    const names = [];
      63 +    let i = 0;
      64 +    const SIG = 0x04034b50;  // local file header signature (little-endian stored as LE UInt32)
      65 +    while (i < buf.length - 30) {
      66 +        if (buf.readUInt32LE(i) === SIG) {
      67 +            const nameLen  = buf.readUInt16LE(i + 26);
      68 +            const extraLen = buf.readUInt16LE(i + 28);
      69 +            const name = buf.slice(i + 30, i + 30 + nameLen).toString('utf8');
      70 +            names.push(name);
      71 +            // Advance past this entry's header + extra + compressed data
      72 +            // We don't decompress — just skip using the compressed size field (offset 18)
      73 +            const compressedSize = buf.readUInt32LE(i + 18);
      74 +            i += 30 + nameLen + extraLen + compressedSize;
      75 +        } else {
      76 +            i++;
      77 +        }
      78 +    }
      79 +    return names;
      80 +}
      81 +

      83      const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: true });
      84      const page = await browser.newPage();

● Write(/mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/PKM/Inbox/MechDraw_Plugin_Spec_2_24_2026.md)
  ⎿  Wrote 330 lines to /mnt/c/Users/User1/ClaudeProjects/PKM/Inbox/MechDraw_Plugin_Spec_2_24_2026.md
        1 # MechDraw AI Plugin — Design Specification
        2
        3 **Status:** Phase X — Future (not yet scheduled)
        4 **Author:** PortolanCAST
        5 **Date:** 2026-02-24
        6 **Related:** PortolanCAST Plugin Loader API (Phase 4B), `plugins.js`, `static/js/plugins/`
        7
        8 ---
        9
       10 ## Summary
       11
       12 MechDraw is a proposed PortolanCAST plugin that converts natural-language duct layout
       13 descriptions into Fabric.js objects on the canvas. The user types a prompt like
       14 "run a 24×12 duct from the AHU northeast 40 feet, then branch left 20 feet" and
       15 MechDraw generates the corresponding Path/Group objects with HVAC markupType metadata.
       16
       17 This spec covers the architecture, integration points, deferred decisions, and security
       18 constraints. It does not commit to a timeline. Implementation requires:
       19
       20 1. A working Ollama instance with a vision-capable model (e.g. llava:7b or mistrall3:8b)
       21 2. A structured output schema stable enough to parse reliably
       22 3. At least one completed review of the PortolanCAST coordinate system
       23
       24 ---
       25
       26 ## Problem Statement
       27
       28 HVAC and mechanical engineers frequently sketch duct layouts on existing architectural
       29 drawings. Current PortolanCAST tools require hand-drawing each duct segment individually.
       30 MechDraw would let a user describe a duct run in plain language, optionally anchored to
       31 visible features in the drawing, and have the tool generate parametric Fabric.js objects.
       32
       33 **Target user story:**
       34 > "I want to say 'run a 24×12 rectangular duct from grid intersection A-3 northeast
       35 > 30 feet, then turn east 15 feet to the diffuser at A-5' and have it appear on the canvas
       36 > without me measuring and clicking."
       37
       38 ---
       39
       40 ## Input Modes
       41
       42 ### Mode 1: Text Prompt (required for v1)
       43
       44 The user types a natural-language description in the plugin panel's text input.
       45 MechDraw parses the prompt using an Ollama LLM with a structured output prompt
       46 that extracts:
       47
       48 ```json
       49 {
       50   "segments": [
       51     {
       52       "type": "duct_rect",
       53       "width_in": 24,
       54       "height_in": 12,
       55       "start": {"x": 0, "y": 0},
       56       "end": {"x": 500, "y": 0},
       57       "label": "Supply Duct — 24×12"
       58     }
       59   ],
       60   "anchors": []
       61 }
       62 ```
       63
       64 Coordinates are in **canvas pixel space** (BASE_DPI=150, same as all other tools).
       65 The plugin must convert real-world dimensions to pixels using the document's
       66 `ScaleManager` (via `app.scale`).
       67
       68 ### Mode 2: Prompt + Floor Plan Image (deferred — v2+)
       69
       70 The user provides both a text prompt AND an image crop from the current PDF view.
       71 Ollama's vision endpoint (`/api/generate` with `images: [base64]`) receives the crop
       72 so the model can spatially anchor the duct run to visible features (grid lines, walls,
       73 equipment labels).
       74
       75 **Deferred reason:** Vision model output is less deterministic. Requires a calibration
       76 strategy and more robust error handling before production use.
       77
       78 ---
       79
       80 ## Output: Fabric.js Objects
       81
       82 MechDraw injects PortolanCAST-compatible Fabric.js objects directly onto the active
       83 canvas page. Each duct segment becomes one or more Fabric objects:
       84
       85 ### Rectangular Duct → `fabric.Rect`
       86
       87 ```javascript
       88 new fabric.Rect({
       89     left: segment.start.x,
       90     top:  segment.start.y,
       91     width:  pixelLength,
       92     height: pixelWidth,
       93     fill: 'transparent',
       94     stroke: '#1565C0',
       95     strokeWidth: 2,
       96     angle: angleDegrees,
       97     // PortolanCAST required metadata
       98     markupType:   'change',    // HVAC additions = change intent by default
       99     markupStatus: 'open',
      100     markupNote:   segment.label,
      101     markupAuthor: 'MechDraw',
      102     markupTimestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
      103 })
      104 ```
      105
      106 ### Duct Run with Label → `fabric.Group`
      107
      108 Complex runs (bends, branches) are grouped with a companion IText label showing
      109 duct size (e.g., "24×12"). The group's `markupType` follows PortolanCAST conventions.
      110
      111 ### Object Injection
      112
      113 Objects are injected using `canvas.stampDefaults(obj)` (applied after construction,
      114 per PortolanCAST convention) followed by `canvas.fabricCanvas.add(obj)`. The plugin
      115 must call `canvas.onContentChange()` after injection to trigger auto-save.
      116
      117 ---
      118
      119 ## Integration: PluginLoader API
      120
      121 MechDraw registers via the existing PluginLoader (Phase 4B):
      122
      123 ```javascript
      124 // In static/js/plugins/mechdraw.js
      125 const MechDraw = {
      126     name: 'mechdraw',
      127     tabLabel: 'MechDraw',
      128
      129     onReady(app) {
      130         this._app = app;
      131         this._renderPanel();
      132     },
      133
      134     onDocumentLoaded(docId) {
      135         this._currentDocId = docId;
      136     },
      137
      138     // Called by toolbar.js when user presses Generate
      139     async generate(prompt) {
      140         const scale = this._app.scale;
      141         const resp  = await fetch('/api/mechdraw/generate', {
      142             method: 'POST',
      143             headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      144             body: JSON.stringify({ prompt, doc_id: this._currentDocId }),
      145         });
      146         const { segments } = await resp.json();
      147         for (const seg of segments) {
      148             this._placeSegment(seg, scale);
      149         }
      150         this._app.canvas.onContentChange();
      151     },
      152
      153     _placeSegment(seg, scale) { /* ... */ },
      154     _renderPanel() { /* inject panel HTML */ },
      155 };
      156
      157 // Register on load
      158 if (window.plugins) {
      159     window.plugins.register(MechDraw);
      160 }
      161 ```
      162
      163 **Lifecycle hooks used:**
      164 - `onReady` — panel render, store `app` reference
      165 - `onDocumentLoaded` — reset prompt, update doc_id
      166
      167 **Lifecycle hooks NOT used (yet):**
      168 - `onPageChanged` — not needed; segments target the active page
      169 - `onMarkupSelected` — not applicable
      170
      171 ---
      172
      173 ## Backend: `/api/mechdraw/generate`
      174
      175 New FastAPI endpoint in `main.py`:
      176
      177 ```python
      178 @app.post("/api/mechdraw/generate")
      179 async def mechdraw_generate(doc_id: int, request: Request):
      180     """
      181     Convert a natural-language duct layout prompt to Fabric.js segment descriptors.
      182
      183     Calls Ollama with a structured output prompt. Returns an array of segment
      184     objects that the frontend converts to Fabric canvas objects.
      185
      186     Security:
      187         - doc_id validated against DB (document must exist)
      188         - prompt is passed to Ollama only (never eval'd or executed)
      189         - Response is JSON-parsed; malformed output returns empty segments
      190     """
      191     body = await request.json()
      192     prompt = str(body.get("prompt", ""))[:1000]   # SECURITY: cap length
      193     ...
      194 ```
      195
      196 **Ollama call pattern:**
      197
      198 ```python
      199 system_prompt = """
      200 You are a mechanical engineering assistant. Convert duct layout descriptions
      201 to JSON segment arrays. Output ONLY valid JSON — no prose, no markdown.
      202
      203 Schema for each segment:
      204 {
      205   "type": "duct_rect",       // always "duct_rect" for now
      206   "width_in": <number>,      // duct width in inches
      207   "height_in": <number>,     // duct height in inches
      208   "start_real": {"x": <ft>, "y": <ft>},   // start in real-world feet
      209   "end_real":   {"x": <ft>, "y": <ft>},   // end in real-world feet
      210   "label": "<string>"
      211 }
      212 """
      213 ```
      214
      215 Coordinate conversion (real feet → canvas pixels):
      216
      217 ```python
      218 def feet_to_pixels(feet: float, paper_inches_per_unit: float, render_dpi: float = 150.0) -> float:
      219     # paper_inches_per_unit: from document scale setting
      220     # RENDER_DPI: the rendering constant (always 150 in PortolanCAST)
      221     return feet * render_dpi * paper_inches_per_unit
      222 ```
      223
      224 ---
      225
      226 ## Coordinate Strategy
      227
      228 All generated objects must respect the PortolanCAST coordinate system:
      229
      230 | Concept | Value |
      231 |---------|-------|
      232 | Render DPI | 150 px/inch |
      233 | Scale setting | `paper_inches_per_unit` from DB |
      234 | Canvas origin | top-left (0,0) |
      235 | Zoom | applied via `Fabric.setZoom(scale)` — objects are in natural coords |
      236
      237 The plugin should read scale from `app.scale.getScale()` (returns `{pixels_per_unit}`),
      238 then multiply real-world distances by `pixels_per_unit` to get canvas pixel values.
      239
      240 **Example:** At 1/4" = 1ft scale, `paper_inches_per_unit = 0.25`.
      241 `pixels_per_unit = 150 × 0.25 = 37.5 px/ft`
      242 A 40ft duct → `40 × 37.5 = 1500 px`
      243
      244 ---
      245
      246 ## Security Constraints
      247
      248 Per PortolanCAST's cybernetic security framework:
      249
      250 1. **Prompt length limit:** cap at 1000 chars server-side (prevent oversized Ollama calls)
      251 2. **Output validation:** JSON-parse the LLM response; catch exceptions; return `{segments: []}`
      252    on any parse failure rather than propagating the error
      253 3. **Coordinate bounds:** clamp generated coordinates to `[0, page_width_px × 2]` —
      254    discard segments outside reasonable canvas bounds
      255 4. **No code execution:** LLM output is never eval'd. Only the JSON segment schema
      256    is consumed; all other fields are ignored.
      257 5. **SECURITY comment on Ollama call:**
      258    ```python
      259    # SECURITY: prompt goes to local Ollama only (127.0.0.1:11434)
      260    # Never send user prompts to external APIs without explicit consent
      261    ```
      262
      263 ---
      264
      265 ## Panel UI (Conceptual)
      266
      267 ```
      268 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      269 │  MechDraw AI                                │
      270 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
      271 │  Describe the duct layout:                  │
      272 │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐   │
      273 │  │ 24x12 supply duct from AHU north 40 │   │
      274 │  │ ft, branch east 20 ft to grille     │   │
      275 │  └─────────────────────────────────────┘   │
      276 │                                             │
      277 │  Start position:  [ x: 0  y: 0  ] (canvas) │
      278 │                                             │
      279 │  [ Generate ]  [ Clear ]                   │
      280 │                                             │
      281 │  ⚠ Uses local Ollama (llava:7b or similar) │
      282 └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      283 ```
      284
      285 ---
      286
      287 ## Open Questions (Deferred)
      288
      289 1. **Model selection:** Which Ollama model produces the most reliable structured JSON?
      290    Candidates: `mistral:7b`, `llama3:8b`, `llava:7b` (vision). Needs benchmarking.
      291
      292 2. **Anchor detection:** How does the user specify "start at grid A-3"? Options:
      293    a. Click-to-anchor before typing (user clicks canvas, plugin captures coordinates)
      294    b. OCR grid labels from the PDF page image and resolve semantically
      295    c. Require explicit pixel coordinates in the prompt (least magical)
      296
      297 3. **Undo integration:** Should MechDraw-generated objects participate in
      298    PortolanCAST's canvas undo/redo stack? They are Fabric objects so they can,
      299    but the history entry should group all segments from one generation as one undo step.
      300
      301 4. **Multi-floor plans:** When the document has multiple pages, MechDraw should
      302    scope generation to the current page only. Cross-page duct runs are out of scope.
      303
      304 5. **Fitting/equipment symbols:** How to represent AHUs, diffusers, VAV boxes?
      305    Could use SVG Paths stored as templates, or plain Rect with IText labels.
      306
      307 ---
      308
      309 ## Integration Checklist (Before Implementation)
      310
      311 - [ ] Confirm Ollama model produces stable JSON output for 10+ test prompts
      312 - [ ] Add `/api/mechdraw/generate` route to PIPELINE.md as Phase X
      313 - [ ] Design start-anchor UI (click-to-set origin point)
      314 - [ ] Write unit tests for `feet_to_pixels()` conversion
      315 - [ ] Write browser tests: prompt → segments → canvas objects count
      316 - [ ] Add `mechdraw` to `plugins.js` HOOK_MAP if new hooks needed
      317 - [ ] Performance test: measure Ollama round-trip latency at 7B/13B model sizes
      318
      319 ---
      320
      321 ## References
      322
      323 - PortolanCAST Plugin Loader API: `static/js/plugins.js`
      324 - PortolanCAST EC Plugin (reference implementation): `static/js/plugins/extended-cognition.js`
      325 - PortolanCAST ScaleManager: `static/js/scale.js`
      326 - Canvas coordinate strategy: `static/js/canvas.js` (BASE_DPI constant)
      327 - Ollama API docs: `http://127.0.0.1:11434/api/generate` (local)
      328 - HVAC duct sizing reference: SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards
      329 - Portolan charts (name origin): Medieval maritime navigation charts (1200s–1600s),
      330   the first technically precise maps — an apt metaphor for PortolanCAST's purpose.

  ---
  Now, on routing PAI and Fabric through Claude Code — let me make sure I understand what you're after. There are a few
  distinct things:

  Fabric (~/go/bin/fabric) currently has OLLAMA_API_URL in its .env and routes all pattern runs to a local Ollama model.
   Fabric supports multiple backends natively — including Anthropic. We can configure it to call the Claude API
  directly.

  PAI is more nuanced. The system has two distinct AI usages:
  1. Text generation (Counsel archetypes, Fabric patterns, analysis) — these can be routed to Claude API
  2. Embeddings for MemoryVault (nomic-embed-text via Ollama) — Claude doesn't offer a standalone embedding API, so this
   part would need to stay on Ollama or switch to a different embedding provider

  When you say "routing through Claude Code" — I want to make sure I understand the goal:

● User answered Claude's questions:
  ⎿  · When you say 'routing through Claude Code', which approach do you mean? → Claude Code's subprocess/SDK

∴ Thinking…

  The user wants to route PAI and Fabric through Claude Code's subprocess/SDK - meaning they want to use the claude CLI
  binary as the LLM backend rather than Ollama.

  Let me think about what this means:

  1. Fabric: Currently uses Ollama. Fabric has vendor support for various backends. We need to figure out if Fabric can
  use the claude CLI or if it needs an OpenAI-compatible API. Looking at the Fabric Go source, it supports: Anthropic,
  OpenAI, Azure, Groq, etc. We could either:
    - Configure Fabric to use Anthropic API directly (with API key)
    - Create a local proxy that wraps claude CLI calls as an OpenAI-compatible API
    - OR: Since the user wants "Claude Code subprocess/SDK" approach, maybe they want to pipe fabric patterns through
  the claude CLI
  2. PAI: The user's Personal AI Infrastructure uses Ollama for text generation in certain skills. To route through
  Claude Code subprocess, we'd need to replace Ollama calls with claude CLI invocations.

  The claude CLI can be invoked in non-interactive mode with a prompt, or with various flags to control output
  formatting and behavior.

  For Fabric, I'm realizing the cleanest path is probably to configure it to use the Anthropic API directly rather than
  trying to wrap the CLI. The claude command-line tool supports system prompts and non-interactive input, which aligns
  with how Fabric passes patterns and user input to LLMs, but Fabric already has native Anthropic support built in.

  The core challenge is that Fabric expects to call an HTTP API, while the claude CLI is a local subprocess—I'd need to
  either create a local OpenAI-compatible server that internally shells out to the CLI, or just use Fabric's existing
  Anthropic integration with a separate API key. The subprocess approach could work for simple cases, but it adds
  unnecessary complexity when Fabric already handles this natively.

  Before committing to any approach, I should verify what the installed claude binary actually supports for
  non-interactive piping, check what vendor configurations Fabric offers, and identify which PAI components are directly
   calling Ollama versus just using Fabric patterns.

● Good. Using the claude CLI as a subprocess backend is smart — it reuses your existing Claude Code auth with no
  separate API key. Let me map what we're working with before proposing a plan:

● Bash(claude --version 2>&1 && echo "---" && claude --help 2>&1 | head -30)
  ⎿  2.1.53 (Claude Code)
     ---
     Usage: claude [options] [command] [prompt]

     Claude Code - starts an interactive session by default, use -p/--print for
     non-interactive output

     Arguments:
       prompt                                            Your prompt

     Options:
       --add-dir <directories...>                        Additional directories to allow tool access to
       --agent <agent>                                   Agent for the current session. Overrides the 'agent'
     setting.
       --agents <json>                                   JSON object defining custom agents (e.g. '{"reviewer":
     {"description": "Reviews code", "prompt": "You are a code reviewer"}}')
       --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions              Enable bypassing all permission checks as an option,
     without it being enabled by default. Recommended only for sandboxes with no internet access.
       --allowedTools, --allowed-tools <tools...>        Comma or space-separated list of tool names to allow (e.g.
      "Bash(git:*) Edit")
       --append-system-prompt <prompt>                   Append a system prompt to the default system prompt
       --betas <betas...>                                Beta headers to include in API requests (API key users
     only)
       --chrome                                          Enable Claude in Chrome integration
       -c, --continue                                    Continue the most recent conversation in the current
     directory
       --dangerously-skip-permissions                    Bypass all permission checks. Recommended only for
     sandboxes with no internet access.
       -d, --debug [filter]                              Enable debug mode with optional category filtering (e.g.,
     "api,hooks" or "!1p,!file")
       --debug-file <path>                               Write debug logs to a specific file path (implicitly
     enables debug mode)
       --disable-slash-commands                          Disable all skills
       --disallowedTools, --disallowed-tools <tools...>  Comma or space-separated list of tool names to deny (e.g.
     "Bash(git:*) Edit")
       --effort <level>                                  Effort level for the current session (low, medium, high)
       --fallback-model <model>                          Enable automatic fallback to specified model when default
     model is overloaded (only works with --print)
       --file <specs...>                                 File resources to download at startup. Format:
     file_id:relative_path (e.g., --file file_abc:doc.txt file_def:img.png)
       --fork-session                                    When resuming, create a new session ID instead of reusing
     the original (use with --resume or --continue)
       --from-pr [value]                                 Resume a session linked to a PR by PR number/URL, or open
     interactive picker with optional search term
       -h, --help                                        Display help for command
       --ide                                             Automatically connect to IDE on startup if exactly one
     valid IDE is available

       --append-system-prompt <prompt>                   Append a system prompt to the default system prompt
       --dangerously-skip-permissions                    Bypass all permission checks. Recommended only for

       --include-partial-messages                        Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works

       --input-format <format>                           Input format (only works with --print): "text" (default),

       --max-budget-usd <amount>                         Maximum dollar amount to spend on API calls (only works
     with --print)
       --model <model>                                   Model for the current session. Provide an alias for the
     latest model (e.g. 'sonnet' or 'opus') or a model's full name (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6').
       --no-session-persistence                          Disable session persistence - sessions will not be saved
     to disk and cannot be resumed (only works with --print)
       --output-format <format>                          Output format (only works with --print): "text" (default),
      "json" (single result), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming) (choices: "text", "json", "stream-json")
       --permission-mode <mode>                          Permission mode to use for the session (choices:
     "acceptEdits", "bypassPermissions", "default", "dontAsk", "plan")
       --plugin-dir <paths...>                           Load plugins from directories for this session only
     (repeatable)
       -p, --print                                       Print response and exit (useful for pipes). Note: The
     workspace trust dialog is skipped when Claude is run with the -p mode. Only use this flag in directories you
     trust.
       --replay-user-messages                            Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout for
     acknowledgment (only works with --input-format=stream-json and --output-format=stream-json)
       --system-prompt <prompt>                          System prompt to use for the session

● Good. Now I have a clear picture. Here's what the architecture looks like and what's actually changeable:

  ---
  What calls Ollama right now:

  ┌────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Component        │    What it uses Ollama for    │            Changeable to Claude?            │
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fabric                 │ Text generation (patterns)    │ ✅ Yes — Fabric has native Anthropic vendor │
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PortolanCAST EC plugin │ AI summary narrative          │ ✅ Yes — one endpoint in main.py            │
  ├────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ MemoryVault            │ Embeddings (nomic-embed-text) │ ⚠️ No — Claude has no embedding API         │
  └────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The key insight: Embeddings have to stay on Ollama (or switch to a different embedding provider — there's no Claude
  embedding endpoint). Everything else — Fabric patterns, PortolanCAST's AI summary, any PAI text generation — can route
   through Claude Code's subprocess.

  Two practical approaches for the claude -p route:

  Option A: Fabric → Anthropic vendor (native, cleanest)
  Fabric has a first-class Anthropic vendor. We just set DEFAULT_VENDOR=Anthropic and provide your API key in .env. This
   uses the API directly, not the CLI subprocess — but it's one config change and works reliably.

  Option B: OpenAI-compatible local proxy wrapping claude -p
  A small Python/Node shim that listens on a local port, accepts OpenAI-style requests, and shells out to claude -p to
  fulfill them. Fabric and PortolanCAST could both point to it. More complex, but reuses Claude Code auth without a
  separate API key.

  The cleanest path depends on one thing: do you already have an Anthropic API key set up, or do you want to
  specifically avoid that and reuse Claude Code's existing auth?

  This is a really interesting architectural question. The user wants to:
  1. Reuse Claude Code's existing auth (no separate API key) for Fabric/PAI
  2. Explore using Ollama models as subordinate agents that Claude Code can delegate work to

  Let me think about both of these:

  Option 1: Claude Code proxy for Fabric/PAI

  The claude -p subprocess approach with an OpenAI-compatible shim is what we need. We could build a small local server
  (FastAPI or similar) that:
  - Listens on a local port (e.g., 11435 to not conflict with Ollama)

  - Accepts OpenAI-format POST requests to /v1/chat/completions
  - Calls claude -p with the system prompt + user message
  - Returns the response in OpenAI format

  Then Fabric points to this as an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

  Option 2: Using Ollama models as agents via Claude Code

  This is trickier - Claude Code can spawn subagents through the Task tool, but those are all Claude models. The user's
  asking if Claude Code can delegate to Ollama models instead, which isn't natively supported through the API, so we'd
  need a workaround like having Claude Code call an Ollama endpoint directly or use a local bridge.

  The cleanest approach would be building an MCP server that wraps Ollama and exposes its models as callable tools,
  since Claude Code has native MCP support built in. Building the MCP server for Ollama...

● This is a great architectural question — and yes, there's a clean way to do both. Let me lay out what I'm seeing:

  ---
  Problem 1: Claude Code auth for Fabric/PAI

  We build a tiny local OpenAI-compatible proxy server (claude-proxy.py) that:
  - Listens on port 11435 (won't conflict with Ollama on 11434)
  - Accepts standard POST /v1/chat/completions requests
  - Shells out to claude -p --no-session-persistence with the prompt
  - Returns a properly-shaped OpenAI JSON response

  Then in Fabric's .env: DEFAULT_VENDOR=LM Studio (or OpenAI) pointing at http://127.0.0.1:11435. Fabric and
  PortolanCAST both route through it with no API key. The proxy reuses your existing Claude Code session auth.

  ---
  Problem 2: Ollama models as subordinate agents

  This is where it gets interesting. Claude Code has native MCP (Model Context Protocol) server support. If we build an
  Ollama MCP server, I gain new callable tools mid-session:

  mcp__ollama__generate(model="mistral:7b", prompt="...")
  mcp__ollama__embed(text="...")
  mcp__ollama__list_models()

  That lets us do tiered reasoning — I stay as the orchestrator for complex work (planning, code, judgment), and I can
  explicitly delegate cheap/mechanical tasks to small local models:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┐
  │           Delegate to Ollama            │    Keep with Claude     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Summarize large docs before I read them │ Architectural decisions │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Bulk text classification                │ Code writing            │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ MemoryVault embeddings (already there)  │ Strategy / counsel      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Repetitive pattern extraction           │ Novel problem solving   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Pre-filtering long logs                 │ Final synthesis         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘

  The important distinction: Claude Code doesn't natively spawn Ollama as agents — I would call the MCP tool explicitly,
   aware of the tradeoff. This is intentional; you don't want a small model silently replacing Claude on important
  tasks.

  ---
  Proposed stack:

  Your Terminal
      ├── fabric → claude-proxy (11435) → claude -p  [text generation]
      ├── PAI skills → claude-proxy (11435) → claude -p  [text generation]
      ├── MemoryVault → Ollama (11434) → nomic-embed-text  [embeddings, stays]
      └── Claude Code session
              └── MCP: ollama-server → Ollama (11434)  [I can delegate to]

  Two things to build:
  1. claude-proxy.py — ~60 lines, FastAPI, runs as a background service
  2. Ollama MCP server — registered in ~/.claude/settings.json under mcpServers

  Want me to build both, or start with one? The proxy is faster (10 min) and unblocks Fabric immediately. The MCP server
   is more interesting architecturally.

        5 -1. Rename the project from "OpenRevu" to "PortolanCAST" across all source files
        6 -2. Add a portable save format (`.portolan` ZIP bundle) for full re-editing across machines
        7 -3. Write MechDraw AI plugin spec to PKM vault (spec only, not implemented)
        4 +**Goal:** Route Fabric/PAI text generation through Claude Code's existing auth; expose
        5 +Ollama local models as MCP tools so Claude can explicitly delegate cheap/bulk work to them.
        6
        7  ---
        8
        9  ## Context
       10
       13 -The project has outgrown its working name. "OpenRevu" implied a clone of Bluebeam
       14 -Revu; "PortolanCAST" is original — portolan charts were the first precise technical
       15 -navigation maps (1200s–1600s), a fitting metaphor for a tool that annotates and
       16 -measures technical drawings. CAST = Computer-Aided Systems Thinking.
       11 +PAI currently uses Ollama (`mistral:7b`) for all text generation. This means every Fabric
       12 +pattern, every AI summary, every analysis goes through a 7B local model. The user wants
       13 +Claude-quality output for everything that matters — without a separate API key — and wants
       14 +the ability to consciously delegate mechanical/bulk work to smaller local models to save cost
       15 +and latency.
       16
       18 -Bundle export solves the "share-for-review" workflow: export a `.portolan` ZIP that
       19 -contains the original PDF plus all markups, layers, and scale settings. The recipient
       20 -opens it like any PDF — the full session is restored exactly.
       17 +**The solution is two components:**
       18 +1. A **Claude Proxy** — an OpenAI-compatible local server that shells out to `claude -p`,
       19 +   letting any OpenAI-API-speaking tool (Fabric, PortolanCAST) use Claude with no API key.
       20 +2. An **Ollama MCP Server** — exposes Ollama models as callable MCP tools so I (Claude Code)
       21 +   can explicitly delegate cheap tasks inline during any session.
       22
       23 +**The delegation doctrine** (to be added to THEDELEGATIONSYSTEM.md):
       24 +
       25 +| Delegate to Ollama (small model) | Keep with Claude |
       26 +|----------------------------------|-----------------|
       27 +| Summarize large docs before reading | Architectural decisions |
       28 +| Bulk text classification | Code writing / implementation |
       29 +| Pattern extraction from repetitive data | Strategy, counsel, synthesis |
       30 +| Pre-filtering long logs/lists | Novel problem solving |
       31 +| MemoryVault embeddings (stays, always) | Final judgment calls |
       32 +
       33  ---
       34
       24 -## Phase 0 — Rename
       35 +## Architecture
       36
       26 -### Scope (source files only — venv/ and node_modules/ excluded)
       37 +```
       38 +Your Terminal
       39 +  ├── fabric → ClaudeProxy (127.0.0.1:11435) → claude -p [Claude models]
       40 +  ├── PortolanCAST EC plugin → ClaudeProxy (11435) → claude -p [Claude models]
       41 +  ├── MemoryVault → Ollama (127.0.0.1:11434) → nomic-embed-text [stays, no change]
       42 +  └── Claude Code session
       43 +        └── MCP: ollama-bridge
       44 +              ├── ollama_list_models()
       45 +              ├── ollama_generate(model, prompt, system_prompt)
       46 +              ├── ollama_summarize(text, model)
       47 +              ├── ollama_classify(text, categories, model)
       48 +              └── memory_search(query, n, collection)
       49 +```
       50

       31 -- `"OpenRevu Team"` → `"PortolanCAST"` (author field in file headers)
       32 -- `"openrevu.db"` → `"portolancast.db"` (database filename in db.py)

       53 +## Component 1: Claude Proxy Server
       54
       61 -### DB path migration note
       55 +**Location:** `~/.claude/tools/ClaudeProxy/`
       56 +**Files:** `server.py`, `run.sh`
       57 +**Runtime:** `~/.claude/MEMORY/venv/bin/python3` (has uvicorn, pydantic, httpx already)
       58 +**Port:** `11435` (no conflict with Ollama 11434 or PortolanCAST 8000)
       59
       63 -`db.py` line 30: `DEFAULT_DB_PATH = Path(__file__).parent / "data" / "openrevu.db"`
       64 -→ change to `portolancast.db`.
       60 +### server.py design
       61
       66 -**Existing local data**: the old `openrevu.db` will no longer be found automatically.
       67 -If the user has existing documents they want to keep, they should rename the file
       68 -manually: `mv data/openrevu.db data/portolancast.db`. Add a note in QUICKSTART.md.
       62 +FastAPI app with two endpoints:
       63
       70 -### Implementation strategy
       64 +**`POST /v1/chat/completions`** — OpenAI-compatible chat
       65 +1. Parse request: `model`, `messages[]`, `stream` (v1 ignores streaming, always sync)
       66 +2. Extract system message (role=system) → `--system-prompt`
       67 +3. Flatten remaining messages into a single prompt string:
       68 +   ```
       69 +   User: {content}
       70 +   Assistant: {content}
       71 +   User: {content}
       72 +   ```
       73 +4. Map model name → claude flag:
       74 +   - `opus*` / `claude-opus*` → `--model opus`
       75 +   - `haiku*` / `claude-haiku*` → `--model haiku`
       76 +   - anything else → `--model sonnet` (safe default)
       77 +5. Call:
       78 +   ```python
       79 +   subprocess.run(
       80 +       ['claude', '-p', '--no-session-persistence',
       81 +        '--output-format', 'text',
       82 +        '--model', model_flag,
       83 +        '--system-prompt', system_text,
       84 +        user_prompt],
       85 +       capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120
       86 +   )
       87 +   ```
       88 +6. Return OpenAI-format JSON response:
       89 +   ```json
       90 +   {
       91 +     "id": "chatcmpl-...",
       92 +     "object": "chat.completion",
       93 +     "choices": [{
       94 +       "index": 0,
       95 +       "message": {"role": "assistant", "content": "<response text>"},
       96 +       "finish_reason": "stop"
       97 +     }],
       98 +     "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
       99 +   }
      100 +   ```
      101
       72 -Use Bash `sed -i` for bulk replacement across source files (fastest approach).
       73 -Run full test suite after rename to confirm nothing broke.
      102 +**`GET /v1/models`** — List available models (required by some clients)
      103 +Returns a static list: `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `claude-opus-4-6`, `claude-haiku-4-5`
      104
      105 +**`GET /health`** — Health check, returns `{"status": "ok"}`
      106 +
      107 +Security:
      108 +- Bind to `127.0.0.1` only (localhost, no network exposure)
      109 +- No auth needed (local-only service)
      110 +- Subprocess timeout: 120s (Claude is sometimes slow on complex prompts)
      111 +
      112 +### run.sh design
      113 +
      114  ```bash
       76 -# Example (run per-file or with find):
       77 -sed -i 's/OpenRevu/PortolanCAST/g; s/openrevu/portolancast/g' main.py db.py ...
      115 +#!/usr/bin/env bash
      116 +# Claude Proxy — run OUTSIDE of any Claude Code session
      117 +# unset CLAUDECODE prevents the nested-session guard from blocking claude -p calls
      118 +unset CLAUDECODE
      119 +exec ~/.claude/MEMORY/venv/bin/python3 ~/.claude/tools/ClaudeProxy/server.py
      120  ```
      121
      122 +The `unset CLAUDECODE` is the documented bypass for the nested session guard. If the user
      123 +starts the proxy from a terminal that has `CLAUDECODE` set (e.g. a terminal opened from
      124 +within Claude Code's Bash tool), this ensures the proxy's `claude -p` calls still work.
      125 +

       82 -## Phase 1 — Bundle Export/Import (.portolan)
      128 +## Component 2: Ollama MCP Server
      129
       84 -### Bundle Format
      130 +**Location:** `~/.claude/mcp-servers/ollama-bridge/`
      131 +**Files:** `server.py`
      132 +**Runtime:** `~/.claude/MEMORY/venv/bin/python3` (after `pip install mcp`)
      133 +**Transport:** stdio (MCP standard — Claude Code launches it automatically)
      134
       86 -`.portolan` is a ZIP (DEFLATED) containing:
      135 +### Prerequisite: Install mcp package
      136
       88 -| File | Contents |
       89 -|------|----------|
       90 -| `metadata.json` | `{version, app:"PortolanCAST", filename, page_count, exported_at}` |
       91 -| `original.pdf` | Byte-exact copy of the source PDF |
       92 -| `markups.json` | `{pages: {"0": fabricJSON, ...}}` — same as markups API |
       93 -| `layers.json` | `{layers:[...], activeId}` — same as layers API |
       94 -| `scale.json` | `{preset, paper_inches_per_unit, label}` — same as scale API |
      137 +```bash
      138 +~/.claude/MEMORY/venv/bin/pip install mcp
      139 +```
      140
       96 -Only `metadata.json` + `original.pdf` required; others are optional (import falls
       97 -back to defaults). This ensures forward/backward compatibility.
      141 +### server.py — 5 tools
      142
       99 -### Backend (main.py)
      143 +**Tool 1: `ollama_list_models`**
      144 +- No input
      145 +- Calls `GET http://127.0.0.1:11434/api/tags`
      146 +- Returns: list of `{name, parameter_size, size_gb}` for each model
      147 +- Description: "List all Ollama models available locally. Use this before delegating to
      148 +  confirm which model to use."
      149
      101 -**New imports** (add to existing imports at top):
      102 -```python
      103 -import io
      104 -import zipfile
      105 -from datetime import datetime
      106 -```
      150 +**Tool 2: `ollama_generate`**
      151 +- Input: `model` (str), `prompt` (str), `system_prompt` (str, optional)
      152 +- Calls `POST /api/generate` with `stream: false`
      153 +- Returns: response text
      154 +- Description: "Run a prompt through a local Ollama model. Use for mechanical tasks that
      155 +  don't require Claude-level reasoning: repetitive extraction, simple Q&A on structured
      156 +  data, format conversion. Available models: mistral:7b (4.3GB), qwen2.5:14b (8.9GB),
      157 +  gemma2:27b (15.6GB)."
      158
      108 -**GET `/api/documents/{doc_id}/export-bundle`**:
      109 -1. Verify doc + PDF file exist
      110 -2. Load markups (`db.get_markups`), layers (`db.get_document_setting('layers')`),
      111 -   scale (`db.get_document_setting('scale')`)
      112 -3. Build in-memory `zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(), 'w', ZIP_DEFLATED)`:
      113 -   - `writestr("metadata.json", ...)` with `datetime.utcnow().isoformat()`
      114 -   - `zf.write(str(pdf_path), "original.pdf")` — reads PDF from disk into ZIP
      115 -   - `writestr("markups.json", json.dumps({pages}))`
      116 -   - `writestr("layers.json", ...)` — raw setting or `_default_layers()`
      117 -   - `writestr("scale.json", ...)` — raw setting or `_default_scale()`
      118 -4. `return Response(buf.getvalue(), media_type="application/zip",
      119 -                   headers={"Content-Disposition": 'attachment; filename="X.portolan"'})`
      159 +**Tool 3: `ollama_summarize`**
      160 +- Input: `text` (str, long input), `model` (str, default `mistral:7b`),
      161 +  `max_words` (int, default 200)
      162 +- Builds summarization system prompt internally
      163 +- Calls `ollama_generate` internally
      164 +- Returns: condensed summary
      165 +- Description: "Summarize a long document or text using a local model. Use this BEFORE
      166 +  reading a large file yourself — get a digest first, then decide if you need the full text."
      167
      121 -Download filename: strip `.pdf` from `doc["filename"]`, append `.portolan`.
      168 +**Tool 4: `ollama_classify`**
      169 +- Input: `text` (str), `categories` (list[str]), `model` (str, default `mistral:7b`)
      170 +- Builds classification prompt: "Classify the following text into exactly one of these
      171 +  categories: {categories}. Reply with only the category name."
      172 +- Returns: category string
      173 +- Description: "Classify text into one of the provided categories using a local model.
      174 +  Good for tagging markups, routing items, sorting logs."
      175
      123 -**POST `/api/documents/import-bundle`**:
      124 -```
      125 -Security checks (same pattern as /api/upload):
      126 -  - Extension must be .portolan
      127 -  - Size <= MAX_UPLOAD_SIZE
      128 -  - zipfile.BadZipFile caught → 400
      176 +**Tool 5: `memory_search`**
      177 +- Input: `query` (str), `n` (int, default 5), `collection` (str, default `all`)
      178 +- Calls `MemoryVault.py search` via subprocess:
      179 +  ```python
      180 +  subprocess.run([VENV_PYTHON, MEMORYVAULT_PY, 'search', query,
      181 +                  '--n', str(n), '--collection', collection], ...)
      182 +  ```
      183 +- Returns: list of results with text + metadata
      184 +- Description: "Search the PAI semantic memory (past sessions, decisions, council outputs).
      185 +  Use this to recall previous conversations, past architectural decisions, or context from
      186 +  earlier work sessions. Always search memory when the user references something from the past."
      187
      130 -Validation:
      131 -  - 'metadata.json' and 'original.pdf' must be in namelist()
      132 -  - PDF bytes must start with b'%PDF-'
      188 +### Why memory_search in MCP is powerful
      189
      134 -Restore sequence:
      135 -  1. Save PDF to PROJECTS_DIR (uuid filename)
      136 -  2. pdf_engine.get_pdf_info() to validate + get page_count
      137 -  3. db.add_document(filename, filepath, page_count, file_size)
      138 -  4. If markups.json → db.save_markups(doc_id, pages)
      139 -  5. If layers.json  → db.set_document_setting(doc_id, 'layers', raw)
      140 -  6. If scale.json   → db.set_document_setting(doc_id, 'scale', raw)
      190 +Currently, to search memory, I have to shell out via Bash. With an MCP tool, I can call it
      191 +inline — it appears in my tool list naturally, and I can search automatically when context
      192 +from past sessions might be relevant, without requiring a separate Bash step.
      193
      142 -Return: {id, filename, page_count, redirect: /edit/{id}}
      143 -```

      301 +   ```bash

      314 +
      315 +3. **MCP tools test** (within Claude Code session):

      317 +   - Call `ollama_list_models` → should return mistral:7b, qwen2.5:14b, gemma2:27b
      318 +   - Call `memory_search("PortolanCAST", 3)` → should return recent session entries

      327  ## Gotchas
      328
      249 -- `datetime` is not currently imported in main.py — add to imports
      250 -- `zf.write(str(pdf_path), "original.pdf")` — first arg must be a string path
      251 -- DB rename: existing `data/openrevu.db` becomes orphaned; note in QUICKSTART
      252 -- `.portolan` import creates a NEW doc (never overwrites existing)
      253 -- `ALLOWED_EXTENSIONS` is `{".pdf"}` — import-bundle has its own extension check inline
      254 -- Phase 0 sed command: run on source files only, NOT on `venv/` or `node_modules/`
      255 -- After rename, test file headers change but test logic is unaffected
      256 -- MEMORY.md references "OpenRevu" — update those too after rename
      329 +- `unset CLAUDECODE` in run.sh is mandatory — without it, `claude -p` fails with nested session error
      330 +- Fabric's `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL` must end in `/v1` — Fabric appends `/chat/completions` itself
      331 +- `OPENAI_API_KEY` must be non-empty in Fabric — use any placeholder string
      332 +- The `mcp` Python package must be installed into MEMORY venv, not system Python (PEP 668)
      333 +- MCP server stdout is the protocol channel — never print to stdout in server.py (use stderr for logs)
      334 +- `memory_search` MCP tool calls MemoryVault.py via subprocess — Ollama must be running for embed
      335 +- PortolanCAST EC plugin: proxy must be running before the AI summary endpoint is called
      336 +- Model name mapping in proxy: be permissive — unknown model names default to sonnet safely
      337 +- Proxy `subprocess.run` timeout should be 120s (long Opus calls can take 30-60s on complex prompts)

  that show text as it streams.

  For the initial version, synchronous blocking is sufficient—I can add streaming later. I'm also noticing they have
